perturbation.original_text: 9.3 Subject to your compliance with the Company's sickness absence procedures (as amended from time to time), the Company may in its sole and absolute discretion pay full salary and contractual benefits during any period of absence due to sickness or injury for up to an aggregate of 3 months in any fifty-two (52) week period (whether such absence is continuous or intermittent in any calendar year). Such payment shall be inclusive of any statutory sick pay due in accordance with applicable legislation in force at the time of absence. The Company may, in its sole and absolute discretion, extend the period of allowance in an individual case if the circumstances so justify. Thereafter, the Company shall pay statutory sick pay or equivalent benefit to which you may be entitled subject to your compliance with the appropriate rules.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.3 Subject to your compliance with the Company's sickness absence procedures (as amended from time to time), the Company may in its sole and absolute discretion pay full salary and contractual benefits during any period of absence due to sickness or injury for up to an aggregate of 3 months in any fifty-two (52) week period (whether such absence is continuous or intermittent in any calendar year). Thereafter, the Company shall pay sick pay or equivalent benefit subject to your compliance with the appropriate rules.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'Such payment shall be inclusive of any statutory sick pay due in accordance with applicable legislation in force at the time of absence' this clause introduces a contradiction with UK law. The original text acknowledges the company's responsibility to provide statutory sick pay.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) under the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 (as amended)
perturbation.law_citation: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the explicit statement that payments will include statutory sick pay, potentially leading the employee to believe they may not receive SSP as required by UK law. The employer is legally obligated to pay SSP to eligible employees, and this omission creates ambiguity and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) : What you'll get - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) Skip contents What you'll get If you’re eligible, you can get £118.75 a week Statutory Sick Pay ( SSP ) for up to 28 weeks. You’ll be paid SSP for all the days you’re off sick that you normally would have worked, except for the first 3. You’ll only be paid SSP for the first 3 working days you are off sick if you received SSP within the last 8 weeks. This must have included a 3-day waiting period before you were paid SSP. How you’re paid SSP is paid by your employer in the same way as your normal wages, for example weekly or monthly. If you have more than one job you may get SSP from each employer. Tax and National Insurance will be deducted. If you think you are not getting the right amount of SSP , talk to your employer. If you’re still not happy, contact the HM Revenue and Customs ( HMRC ) enquiry line . View a printable version of the whole guide Related content Is this page useful? perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit mention of statutory sick pay (SSP) being included, which contradicts the legal obligation for employers to provide SSP as outlined in the provided snippet from GOV.UK. The snippet states, 'If you’re eligible, you can get £118.75 a week Statutory Sick Pay ( SSP ) for up to 28 weeks. You’ll be paid SSP for all the days you’re off sick that you normally would have worked, except for the first 3.' By omitting the reference to SSP, the modified contract creates a situation where the employee might incorrectly assume they are not entitled to receive SSP, thereby violating the employer's legal obligation to provide it. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, where essential information is removed, leading to potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 In addition to the usual public holidays you will be entitled to 25 working days paid holiday in each calendar year. The holiday will accrue on a pro rata basis throughout each calendar year.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 In addition to the usual public holidays you will be entitled to 25 working days holiday in each calendar year. The holiday will accrue on a pro rata basis throughout each calendar year.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'paid' before 'holiday' implies that the employee might not be paid for their holiday time, creating a contradiction with the Working Time Regulations 1998 in the UK, which mandates paid holiday.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Working Time Regulations 1998 require employers to provide paid holiday. By removing the word 'paid,' the contract suggests the holiday may be unpaid, contradicting UK law. This creates legal risk for the company as it could be in violation of employment law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Holiday entitlement: Entitlement - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Holiday entitlement Skip contents Entitlement Almost all people classed as workers are legally entitled to 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year (known as statutory leave entitlement or annual leave). This includes: agency workers workers with ‘irregular hours’ (where the number of hours they work in a pay period often or always changes) ‘part-year’ workers (where there are periods of at least a week in a leave year where they do not need to work and are not paid) An employer can include bank holidays as part of statutory annual leave. Statutory annual leave entitlement Most workers who work a 5-day week must receive at least 28 days’ paid annual leave a year. This is the equivalent of 5.6 weeks of holiday. Working part-time Part-time workers who work regular hours for the whole year are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday, but this will amount to fewer than 28 days. For example, if they work 3 days a week, they must get at least 16.8 days’ leave a year (3 × 5.6). Use the holiday entitlement calculator to work out a part-time worker’s leave. Working irregular hours or for part of the year People working irregular hours or part of the year are entitled to up to 5.6 weeks statutory leave. They will build up (‘accrue’) leave depending on the hours they’ve already worked, rather than getting a fixed number of days or hours. Use the holiday entitlement calculator to work out how much leave they’ve accrued in a pay period. For leave years beginning on or before 31 March 2024, leave entitlement for irregular-hours and part-year workers does not need to be accrued based on hours they’ve already worked. You can use the holiday entitlement calculator to estimate their entitlement based on the average days or hours they work each week. Limits on statutory leave Statutory paid holiday entitlement is limited to 28 days. For example, staff working 6 days a week are only entitled to 28 days’ paid holiday. Bank holidays Bank or public holidays do not have to be given as paid leave. An employer can choose to include bank holidays as part of a worker’s statutory annual leave. Extra leave An employer can choose to offer more leave than the legal minimum. They do not have to apply all the rules that apply to statutory leave to the extra leave. For example, a worker might need to be employed for a certain amount of time before they become entitled to it. Other aspects of holiday entitlement Workers have the right to: get holiday pay build up holiday entitlement during certain types of leave, such as maternity, paternity, or adoption leave build up holiday entitlement while off work sick request holiday at the same time as sick leave Disputes Paid annual leave is a legal right that an employer must provide. If a worker thinks their rights to leave and pay are not being met there are a number of ways to resolve the dispute . View a printable version of the whole guide Related content Is this page useful? perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the word 'paid' from 'paid holiday', which directly contradicts the legal requirement for paid holiday as stated in the scraped snippet 2 from GOV.UK: 'Almost all people classed as workers are legally entitled to 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year'. This omission creates a legal loophole where the employee might not be paid for their holiday time, making the contract non-compliant with UK employment law.

perturbation.original_text: 18 DATA PROTECTION Processing of personal data and our policies 18.1 Information relating to an individual (or from which an individual may be identified) is called "personal data". 18.2 In processing personal data, we are required to comply with the law on data protection. To help us achieve this, we have produced a privacy notice ("Privacy Notice"). This may be found in the Employee Handbook. You must read this and comply with it in carrying out your work. Data protection principles 18.3 In complying with the law on data protection, we are required to comply with what are known as data protection principles. These are summarised in our Privacy Notice. In performing your role and carrying out your responsibilities, you must do your best to ensure that we comply with these principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 18 DATA PROTECTION Processing of personal data and our policies 18.1 Information relating to an individual (or from which an individual may be identified) is called "personal data". 18.2 In processing personal data, we may comply with data protection principles as outlined in the Employee Handbook. In performing your role and carrying out your responsibilities, you should endeavour to comply with these principles.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "we are required to comply with the law on data protection" to "we may comply with data protection principles," and changing "you must do your best to ensure that we comply with these principles" to "you should endeavor to comply with these principles," the revised clause introduces a significant legal contradiction. It weakens the obligation to adhere to data protection laws, specifically the GDPR, which has implications for UK companies processing personal data.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 5 mandates that personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently. By suggesting compliance with data protection principles is optional ("may comply") and only requiring an endeavor rather than a strict obligation, the modified text contradicts the core principles of GDPR, potentially leading to legal liabilities for the company.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK GDPR guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content UK GDPR guidance and resources Back to top
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with data protection laws, whereas the changed text makes it optional by using "may comply." Furthermore, the original text states that the employee 'must' do their best to ensure compliance, but the changed text softens this requirement to 'should endeavor'. This weakening of the obligation directly contradicts the intent of data protection laws. The omission of the strict requirement to comply and ensure compliance creates a legal loophole where adherence to data protection principles becomes discretionary rather than mandatory, falling under the definition of an Omission Legal Contradiction.
